summary: The legendary unification of Upper and Lower Egypt under Narmer (Menes), establishing the First Dynasty and one of history's earliest states.
Sargon of Akkad conquered the Sumerian city-states, creating history's first known empire stretching from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean.
Centuries of intermittent warfare between Elam and Babylon over Mesopotamian lowlands. Elamites sacked Babylon and carried off the stele of Hammurabi before being destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar I.
Semitic Hyksos people conquered Lower Egypt, introducing the horse chariot and composite bow. Their expulsion by the Theban pharaohs launched Egypt's New Kingdom era.
New Kingdom pharaohs conquered deep into Nubia, extracting gold and slaves. Egyptian forts and temples at Abu Simbel projected power into the African interior.
Thutmose I expanded Egypt's borders further than any previous pharaoh, campaigning south into Nubia and north to the Euphrates River, establishing Egyptian imperial ambitions.
Egyptian pharaoh Thutmose III defeated a Canaanite coalition near Megiddo, establishing Egyptian hegemony over the Levant. One of the earliest battles with detailed records.
A century of Egyptian-Hittite rivalry over control of Syria and the Levant, culminating in the Battle of Kadesh. Ended with the first known international peace treaty.
Largest chariot battle in history between Egypt and the Hittites. Ended in tactical stalemate and the first known international peace treaty.
Semi-legendary conflict between Mycenaean Greeks and the city of Troy. Archaeological evidence at Hisarlik suggests a historical basis for the Homeric narrative.
Mysterious coalition of maritime raiders attacked civilisations across the Eastern Mediterranean, contributing to the Bronze Age Collapse that ended multiple empires.

canonical_name: Ramesses III vs the Sea Peoples
summary: Ramesses III defeated a massive invasion of Sea Peoples in the Nile Delta, both on land and sea. Egypt survived the Bronze Age Collapse but was permanently weakened.
A coalition of Levantine states including Israel and Damascus halted Assyrian expansion at Qarqar. One of the earliest battles attested in multiple ancient sources.
Nubian pharaohs of the 25th Dynasty conquered and ruled all of Egypt, the only time a sub-Saharan African power controlled the ancient Mediterranean superpower.
Assyria under Shalmaneser V and Sargon II conquered the northern Kingdom of Israel, deporting its population. One of history's earliest documented ethnic cleansings.
Sennacherib campaigned against Babylon and besieged Jerusalem. He destroyed Babylon in 689 BCE but famously failed to take Jerusalem, an event recorded in the Bible.
Ashurbanipal, the last great Assyrian king, conquered Egypt and destroyed Elam. Despite military successes, the empire began its fatal decline after his death.
Coalition of Babylonians and Medes destroyed the Neo-Assyrian Empire, sacking Nineveh in 612 BCE. One of history's most powerful empires was utterly annihilated.
Nebuchadnezzar II conquered Jerusalem, destroyed Solomon's Temple, and deported the Jewish elite to Babylon — the Babylonian Captivity that profoundly shaped Judaism.
War between the Medes and Lydians ended by a solar eclipse (predicted by Thales) during the Battle of the Eclipse. Both sides interpreted it as a divine sign to make peace.
Cyrus the Great conquered Babylon, reportedly welcomed as a liberator. He freed the Jews from Babylonian Captivity and issued the Cyrus Cylinder, an early declaration of rights.
Persia conquered Egypt at the Battle of Pelusium, incorporating it into the world's largest empire. Egypt would not be fully independent again for over 2,000 years.
Greek city-states of Ionia revolted against Persian rule with Athenian support. Its suppression provoked Darius to invade mainland Greece, leading to the Greco-Persian Wars.
Athenian hoplites repelled a Persian invasion force on the plain of Marathon, preserving Greek independence and inspiring the legend of the marathon run.
A small Greek force led by 300 Spartans held the narrow pass at Thermopylae against Xerxes' massive army for three days before being outflanked.
Greek fleet under Themistocles destroyed the Persian navy in the straits of Salamis, turning the tide of the Greco-Persian Wars.
Combined Greek forces destroyed the remaining Persian army in Boeotia, decisively ending Xerxes' invasion of Greece and securing Greek independence.
Two and a half centuries of warfare among Chinese kingdoms, ending with Qin's unification of China. Produced Sun Tzu's Art of War and vast casualty figures.
Three-decade war between Athens and Sparta that devastated Greece. Ended Athenian imperial ambitions and weakened the Greek city-states.
Athens sent a massive expedition to conquer Syracuse during the Peloponnesian War. The total destruction of the fleet and army was the worst military disaster in Athenian history.
Greek states allied with Persia against Spartan hegemony. Ended with the King's Peace, which ironically gave Persia authority over Greek affairs.
Theban general Epaminondas shattered Spartan military supremacy using the oblique order formation. Ended Sparta's 200-year military dominance of Greece.
Three wars between Rome and the Samnites for control of central Italy. Roman victory established Rome as the dominant power on the Italian peninsula.
Philip II of Macedon's victory over the combined Greek city-states ended Greek independence. His son Alexander, aged 18, commanded the cavalry that broke the Theban Sacred Band.
Alexander the Great's second major victory over Darius III in a narrow coastal plain in modern Turkey. Captured the Persian royal family.
Alexander built a causeway to the island fortress of Tyre and captured it after a seven-month siege. 6,000 defenders were killed, 30,000 enslaved.
Alexander the Great's decisive victory over the Persian Empire near modern Mosul. Ended the Achaemenid dynasty and opened the East to Hellenistic civilization.
Alexander's easternmost major battle, fought against King Porus on the banks of the Jhelum River in modern Pakistan. Marked the limit of Macedonian expansion.
Alexander the Great's generals fought for decades over his empire, eventually dividing it into Ptolemaic Egypt, the Seleucid Empire, and Antigonid Macedon.

canonical_name: Maurya conquest of the Ganges plain
summary: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty and unified most of the Indian subcontinent, founding the Maurya Empire with guidance from the strategist Kautilya.
Chandragupta Maurya defeated Seleucus I's invasion of India, gaining the Hindu Kush and Balochistan regions. Sealed by a peace treaty and a marriage alliance.
King Pyrrhus of Epirus won costly victories against Rome in southern Italy, giving rise to the term 'Pyrrhic victory.' He ultimately withdrew, leaving Rome dominant.
Rome and Carthage fought for control of Sicily in a war dominated by naval battles. Rome built its first major fleet and won control of the western Mediterranean.
Emperor Ashoka's bloody conquest of Kalinga caused such suffering that it famously converted him to Buddhism and non-violence, transforming the Mauryan Empire.
The Parthians gradually conquered Iran and Mesopotamia from the Seleucids, establishing a major empire that would rival Rome for centuries.
Ptolemaic Egypt's last great military victory, using Egyptian native soldiers for the first time. The largest Hellenistic battle showed the declining power of Alexander's successor states.
Hannibal's double envelopment annihilated a Roman army of 80,000 men. Considered one of the greatest tactical masterpieces in military history.
Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal in North Africa, ending the Second Punic War. Rome became the dominant power of the western Mediterranean.
The Han Dynasty fought the Xiongnu nomadic confederacy for over a century, eventually breaking their power. The campaigns led to the opening of the Silk Road.
Rome decisively defeated the Seleucid Empire in Asia Minor, establishing Roman dominance over the eastern Mediterranean. Antiochus lost all territory west of the Taurus Mountains.
Jewish revolt against Seleucid attempts to suppress Judaism. The rededication of the Temple is celebrated as Hanukkah. Established the Hasmonean dynasty.
Rome besieged and completely destroyed Carthage in the Third Punic War. The city was razed, population killed or enslaved, ending Carthaginian civilization.
Emperor Wu of Han conquered the Korean kingdom of Wiman Joseon, establishing four commanderies and extending Chinese cultural influence over the Korean peninsula.
The great city of Teotihuacan dominated central Mexico through military and economic power, influencing Maya and Zapotec civilisations across Mesoamerica.
Rome's Italian allies revolted demanding citizenship. Though Rome won militarily, it granted citizenship to all Italians, transforming the Roman state.
Sulla marched on Rome twice, establishing a dictatorship and proscribing thousands. The first time Roman legions were turned against Rome itself.
Mithridates VI of Pontus fought Rome in three wars, initially massacring 80,000 Romans in Asia Minor. His final defeat by Pompey secured Roman control of the Eastern Mediterranean.
Gladiator Spartacus led history's largest slave revolt, defeating several Roman armies before being crushed by Crassus. Six thousand survivors were crucified along the Appian Way.
Julius Caesar's eight-year campaign to conquer Gaul. Ancient sources claim a million dead and another million enslaved, reshaping western Europe permanently.
Parthian horse archers annihilated Crassus' Roman legions in the Syrian desert. Rome's worst defeat in the east and the end of the First Triumvirate.
Caesar crossed the Rubicon and defeated Pompey's forces across the Mediterranean. Led to Caesar's dictatorship and the end of the Roman Republic.
Naval battle that ended the Roman Republic's civil wars. Octavian's victory led to the establishment of the Roman Empire.
Germanic tribes ambushed and destroyed three Roman legions in the forest. Rome never again attempted to conquer Germany east of the Rhine.
Roman invasion of Britain under Emperor Claudius, gradually conquering England and Wales. Boudica's revolt in 60 CE briefly threatened to expel the Romans before being crushed.
British queen Boudica led a massive revolt against Rome, burning London, Colchester, and St Albans. The Romans crushed the revolt and Boudica died, but she became a British national icon.
Series of revolts by Jews against Roman rule, culminating in the destruction of the Second Temple (70 CE) and the Bar Kokhba revolt. Led to the Jewish diaspora.
After Nero's suicide, four emperors seized power in rapid succession through military coups. Vespasian ultimately prevailed, founding the Flavian dynasty.
Trajan conquered Dacia in two campaigns, seizing its gold mines and colonizing the province. Commemorated by Trajan's Column in Rome.
Trajan conquered Mesopotamia, briefly pushing the Roman Empire to its greatest extent. The conquests were abandoned under Hadrian.
China fragmented into three competing kingdoms after the fall of the Han dynasty. One of the deadliest periods in human history, dramatised in Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
Four centuries of intermittent warfare between Rome/Byzantium and Sassanid Persia over Mesopotamia and Armenia. Exhausted both empires before the Arab conquests.
Fifty years of civil war, plague, and invasion nearly destroyed the Roman Empire. Over 50 emperors claimed the throne before Diocletian restored order.
Emperor Aurelian defeated the breakaway Gallic Empire, reunifying the Roman Empire after the Crisis of the Third Century. Aurelian earned the title 'Restorer of the World.'
Northern China fragmented into sixteen kingdoms ruled by non-Chinese peoples. Mass slaughter and ethnic conflict devastated the population of the Yellow River region.
Samudragupta conquered much of the Indian subcontinent, earning comparison to Alexander. The Gupta period became India's Classical Golden Age of science and art.
The Huns swept into Europe from the Central Asian steppe, pushing Germanic peoples into the Roman Empire. Under Attila, they terrorised Europe before his death in 453 ended the threat.
Gothic cavalry annihilated a Roman army and killed Emperor Valens. Marked the beginning of the end for the Western Roman Empire.
Alaric I's Visigoths sacked Rome for the first time in 800 years, shocking the ancient world and symbolising the decline of Roman power.
The Vandals conquered Roman North Africa, seizing Carthage and creating a powerful kingdom. From their naval bases they raided Rome itself, controlling the western Mediterranean.
Combined Roman and Visigothic forces halted Attila the Hun's invasion of Gaul. One of the last major Roman military victories.
Vandals looted Rome for two weeks, carrying off treasures from the Temple of Jerusalem. The word 'vandalism' derives from this event.

canonical_name: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
summary: Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last Western Roman Emperor Romulus Augustulus in Ravenna, conventionally marking the end of antiquity.
Emperor Justinian attempted to restore the Roman Empire by reconquering Italy from the Ostrogoths. The devastating wars depopulated the Italian peninsula.
Justinian's reconquest of Italy from the Ostrogoths devastated the Italian peninsula through 20 years of warfare, famine, and plague. Rome's population dropped to 30,000.
The Sui Dynasty reunified China after 300 years of division between north and south. The brief but dynamic Sui laid the groundwork for the Tang golden age.
Sui China launched massive invasions of Korean kingdom Goguryeo, all ending in catastrophic failure. The military disasters contributed to the Sui dynasty's collapse.
The final and most devastating war between Rome and Persia. Heraclius brilliantly counterattacked, but both empires were so exhausted they fell easy prey to the Arab conquests.
The Muslim conquest ended the 400-year Sasanian Empire and brought Islam to Persia, fundamentally transforming one of the world's oldest civilizations.
Arab Muslim armies conquered Syria, Palestine, and the Levant from the Byzantine Empire, permanently changing the religious and linguistic character of the region.
The Turkic Khazars blocked Arab expansion into Eastern Europe for over a century, fighting major wars in the Caucasus. One of the most strategically significant but forgotten conflicts.
Arab armies conquered the entirety of North Africa from the Byzantines and Berbers, Islamising and Arabising the region from Egypt to Morocco.
Civil war over the succession to the Prophet Muhammad, including the battles of the Camel and Siffin. Created the permanent Sunni-Shia schism in Islam.
Centuries of warfare between Byzantium and Bulgaria for control of the Balkans. Emperor Basil II 'the Bulgar-Slayer' conquered Bulgaria in 1018, blinding 15,000 prisoners.
Muslim armies from North Africa conquered nearly all of the Iberian Peninsula from the Visigoths, establishing Al-Andalus and beginning 700 years of Muslim presence in Spain.
Arab conquest of Sindh (modern Pakistan) marked the beginning of Islamic expansion into the Indian subcontinent. Arab rule introduced Islam to South Asia.
Massive Arab siege of Constantinople defeated by Greek fire and a harsh winter. Saved the Byzantine Empire and Christian Europe from Muslim conquest for centuries.
Traditional starting point of the Reconquista. A small Christian force defeated a Muslim army in the mountains of northern Spain, founding the Kingdom of Asturias.
Frankish forces halted the northward advance of the Umayyad Caliphate into western Europe, preserving Christian dominance north of the Pyrenees.
The Abbasid revolution overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate in a bloody civil war. The Abbasids moved the capital to Baghdad, inaugurating the Islamic Golden Age.
One of the deadliest conflicts in history, this rebellion devastated Tang China. Population censuses suggest a loss of two-thirds of the population in affected areas.
Charlemagne waged 30 years of war to conquer and forcibly Christianize the pagan Saxons, including the Massacre of Verden where 4,500 Saxon prisoners were beheaded.
Norse raiders devastated monasteries and settlements across England beginning with Lindisfarne in 793, eventually establishing the Danelaw across eastern England.
Alfred the Great and his successors gradually reconquered the Danelaw from the Vikings, unifying England under a single Anglo-Saxon kingdom by 954.
Vikings raided deep into Francia, besieging Paris in 845 and 885-886. Ended with the establishment of Norse-ruled Normandy in 911.
Norse Varangians established rule over Slavic populations along the Dnieper trade route, founding the Kievan Rus state that became the precursor to Russia and Ukraine.
Magyar horsemen raided deep into France, Italy, and Germany for 50 years. Their defeat at the Battle of Lechfeld in 955 ended the raids and led to Hungary's Christianization.
Otto I's decisive victory over the Magyars ended 50 years of Hungarian raids into Western Europe. Led to Otto's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor and Hungary's conversion to Christianity.
Mahmud of Ghazni raided northern India seventeen times, sacking temples including the wealthy Somnath temple. Brought Islam deeper into the Indian subcontinent.
The south Indian Chola Empire launched a naval expedition against the maritime Srivijaya Empire in Southeast Asia, demonstrating Indian naval power projection across the Indian Ocean.
Almoravid Berbers invaded the Ghana Empire in West Africa, weakening it and spreading Islam throughout the Sahel. Led to the empire's fragmentation.
Centuries of Turkish conquest gradually turned Anatolia from a Greek-speaking Christian heartland into a Turkish Muslim one. Culminated in the rise of the Ottoman state.
Norman adventurers conquered Muslim-ruled Sicily, creating a remarkably multicultural kingdom where Latin, Greek, and Arabic cultures flourished together.
William the Conqueror defeated King Harold at Hastings, reshaping English language, law, and culture. One of history's most consequential single battles.
Seljuk Turks defeated and captured the Byzantine emperor, opening Anatolia to Turkish settlement. Began the transformation of Asia Minor from Greek to Turkish.
Western European knights captured Jerusalem and established crusader states in the Levant. The siege of Jerusalem ended in a massacre of Muslim and Jewish residents.
Crusaders stormed Jerusalem and massacred Muslim and Jewish inhabitants. Established the Kingdom of Jerusalem, a crusader state that lasted until 1291.
Muhammad of Ghor conquered northern India, defeating Prithviraj III at Tarain in 1192. Established the Delhi Sultanate, beginning centuries of Muslim rule in India.
Champa sacked the Khmer capital Angkor in 1177. King Jayavarman VII expelled them and rebuilt Angkor Thom, creating some of Southeast Asia's greatest monuments.
Saladin destroyed the Crusader army near the Sea of Galilee, capturing the True Cross. Jerusalem fell soon after, triggering the Third Crusade.
Richard the Lionheart's campaign to recapture Jerusalem from Saladin. Ended with a negotiated truce granting Christian pilgrims access to Jerusalem.
Crusading orders conquered and forcibly Christianized the Baltic peoples of modern Latvia, Estonia, and Lithuania. The last pagan regions of Europe were subjugated.
Crusaders diverted from the Holy Land to sack Constantinople, the greatest Christian city. Fatally weakened the Byzantine Empire three centuries before its fall.
Genghis Khan unified the Mongol tribes and launched devastating campaigns against northern China's Jin Dynasty and the Western Xia, beginning the largest contiguous empire in history.
Successive Turkish and Afghan dynasties ruled from Delhi, extending Muslim power across much of the subcontinent until Timur's devastating sack of Delhi in 1398.
Crusade against the Cathar heresy in southern France, destroying Languedoc's distinct culture. At Beziers, the papal legate allegedly said: 'Kill them all, God will know his own.'

canonical_name: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
summary: Decisive Christian victory over the Almohads in Spain. Broke Muslim military power in Iberia and opened southern Spain to the Reconquista.
Crusaders attacked Egypt via the port of Damietta, hoping to trade it for Jerusalem. The campaign bogged down in the Nile Delta and ended in defeat.
Genghis Khan's devastating invasion of Central Asia in retaliation for the murder of Mongol envoys. Entire cities were razed and populations massacred.
Decades-long Mongol conquest of southern China, completing the subjugation of all China under Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty. One of the deadliest wars in history.
Sundiata Keita's victory at the Battle of Kirina founded the Mali Empire, which became the richest state in Africa under Mansa Musa, controlling Saharan gold trade.
Mongol forces swept through Russia, Poland, and Hungary, annihilating armies at Legnica and Mohi. The invasion reshaped Eastern European politics for centuries.
Mongols destroyed Baghdad, ending the Abbasid Caliphate and devastating the Islamic Golden Age's greatest city. Libraries, hospitals, and mosques were destroyed.
Mamluk Egypt inflicted the first major defeat on the Mongols, halting their advance into Africa and the Middle East. A turning point in world history.
Two Mongol invasion fleets were destroyed by typhoons (kamikaze, 'divine wind') off Japan. The failed invasions became central to Japanese national mythology.
The Mamluks stormed Acre, the last major Crusader stronghold in the Holy Land. Its fall ended two centuries of Crusader presence in the Levant.
The Majapahit Empire dominated the Indonesian archipelago through naval power and tributary diplomacy, claiming sovereignty over much of modern Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines.
Scotland fought for independence from English domination. William Wallace's guerrilla campaign and Robert the Bruce's victory at Bannockburn (1314) secured Scottish sovereignty.
Robert the Bruce's decisive victory over a much larger English army near Stirling. Secured Scottish independence and became a defining moment of Scottish national identity.
Dynastic conflict between England and France lasting 116 years. Featured longbow dominance at Agincourt and Joan of Arc's campaigns. Ended English continental ambitions.
English longbowmen devastated French cavalry in the first major battle of the Hundred Years War. Demonstrated the dominance of missile infantry over mounted knights.
Centuries of warfare between Hindu Vijayanagara and the Muslim Deccan sultanates for control of southern India. The Battle of Talikota in 1565 destroyed Vijayanagara.
Over a century the Ottomans conquered the entire Balkan peninsula, transforming the region's demographics, religion, and culture for 500 years.
English forces under the Black Prince captured the French king John II. A humiliating French defeat that plunged France into political crisis.
Peasant uprising in northern France during the Hundred Years War, driven by crushing taxation and noble incompetence. Brutally suppressed by the nobility.
Tamerlane conquered from Turkey to India, leaving pyramids of skulls outside defeated cities. His campaigns devastated Central Asia and the Middle East.
Major uprising triggered by poll taxes. Rebels marched on London and briefly held the city before Wat Tyler was killed during negotiations with the king.
Ottoman victory over the Serbs at Kosovo Polje. Both Sultan Murad I and Prince Lazar were killed. Became a foundational myth of Serbian national identity.
Ottoman victory over the last major Crusade, destroying a combined European army. Established Ottoman dominance in the Balkans for centuries.
Timur sacked Baghdad for the second time, ordering each soldier to bring him a head. The city that had been the jewel of the Islamic world was reduced to ruins.
Timur defeated and captured Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I, delaying Ottoman expansion by 50 years. The Ottoman Interregnum followed as Bayezid's sons fought for the throne.
Outnumbered English archers devastated French cavalry in the mud. Henry V's greatest victory and one of history's most famous battles.
Czech followers of Jan Hus defeated multiple Catholic crusades using innovative wagon-fortress tactics. A proto-Protestant movement that foreshadowed the Reformation.
The Aztec Triple Alliance dominated central Mexico through military conquest and tributary extraction, waging constant wars including 'Flower Wars' to capture sacrificial victims.
Pachacuti transformed the Inca state from a small kingdom into the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, conquering from Ecuador to Chile through military might and diplomacy.
The last serious crusade to stop Ottoman expansion in Europe. King Vladislav III was killed at the Battle of Varna, ending hopes of a Christian reconquest of the Balkans.
Ottomans destroyed the last Crusader army to threaten them in Europe. King Wladyslaw of Poland-Hungary was killed, ending hopes of driving the Ottomans from Europe.
Ottoman forces breached Constantinople's walls with gunpowder artillery, ending the 1,100-year Byzantine Empire and the last remnant of Rome.
Dynastic civil wars for the English throne between the houses of York and Lancaster. Ended with Henry Tudor's victory at Bosworth Field and the founding of the Tudor dynasty.

canonical_name: War of the Roses — Battle of Towton
summary: The bloodiest battle ever fought on English soil, fought in a snowstorm. Around 28,000 men died. Edward IV seized the throne from Henry VI.
The Songhai Empire conquered the declining Mali Empire and became the largest state in African history. Destroyed by a Moroccan invasion using gunpowder in 1591.
Succession dispute in the Ashikaga shogunate destroyed Kyoto and triggered Japan's century-long Sengoku (Warring States) period of civil warfare.

canonical_name: Crimean Khanate raids on Eastern Europe
summary: Three centuries of slave raids from the Crimea into Eastern Europe. An estimated 2-3 million people were captured and sold into slavery in the Ottoman Empire.
The final phase of the 700-year Christian reconquest of the Iberian Peninsula from Muslim rule, ending with the surrender of Granada's last Moorish king.
Henry Tudor defeated and killed Richard III, ending the Wars of the Roses and founding the Tudor dynasty. Richard was the last English king killed in battle.
Afonso de Albuquerque captured Goa, making it the capital of Portuguese India. The city became a major trading hub and center of Portuguese colonial power in Asia for 450 years.
Portugal seized the strategic port of Malacca, controlling the spice trade between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea. A key step in European global maritime dominance.

canonical_name: Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire
summary: Hernán Cortés and indigenous allies conquered the Aztec Empire. Disease killed far more than combat, devastating the population of central Mexico.
Babur's descendants built one of history's wealthiest empires across the Indian subcontinent, synthesising Persian, Turkic, and Indian cultures.
Babur's gunpowder-armed army defeated the much larger forces of the Delhi Sultanate. Founded the Mughal Empire that would rule most of India for three centuries.
Ottoman victory that destroyed the Kingdom of Hungary. King Louis II drowned fleeing the battlefield. Hungary was partitioned between the Ottomans and Habsburgs for 150 years.
Ottoman armies besieged Vienna at the height of Ottoman expansion into Europe. The siege's failure marked the northwestern limit of Ottoman power.
War of succession between Inca brothers Atahualpa and Huascar fatally weakened the empire just as Pizarro arrived. Atahualpa won but was captured by the Spanish.
The Adal Sultanate nearly conquered Christian Ethiopia with Ottoman support. Portuguese musketeers intervened to save the Ethiopian empire in one of Africa's largest pre-colonial wars.

canonical_name: Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire
summary: Francisco Pizarro exploited an Inca civil war to conquer the largest empire in the Americas. Combined with epidemic disease, the population collapsed.
Decades of war between the Ottoman and Safavid empires over Mesopotamia and the Caucasus. Part of a centuries-long Sunni-Shia geopolitical rivalry.
The Mapuche resisted Spanish and later Chilean conquest for over 300 years, the longest sustained indigenous resistance in the Americas. Never fully conquered by Spain.
The teenage Akbar's forces defeated Hemu's army, securing Mughal control over northern India. Akbar went on to become the greatest Mughal emperor.
Ivan the Terrible's failed attempt to conquer Livonia (modern Estonia/Latvia) for access to the Baltic Sea. Left Russia weakened and contributed to the Time of Troubles.
Decades of civil war between French Catholics and Huguenot Protestants. The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of 1572 killed thousands of Huguenots across France.
Combined Deccan sultanates destroyed the great Hindu Vijayanagara Empire at the Battle of Talikota. The magnificent capital city was sacked and never reoccupied.
The Netherlands fought for independence from Spain in a protracted war driven by religion and economics. Ended with Dutch independence and the rise of Dutch global trade.
The Kingdom of Kongo faced invasions, civil wars, and Portuguese interference that gradually fragmented this once-powerful Central African state.
Massive galley battle in the Gulf of Patras where the Holy League destroyed the Ottoman fleet. Halted Ottoman naval expansion in the western Mediterranean.
Mass killing of French Protestants beginning in Paris and spreading to other cities. One of the worst religious massacres in European history, shocking the Protestant world.

Cossack leader Yermak led a small force across the Urals and conquered the Khanate of Sibir, opening Siberia to Russian colonisation. Yermak drowned in 1585, but Russian expansion continued relentlessly eastward to the Pacific.
Spain's massive invasion fleet was defeated by English ships and storms. Ended Spain's dream of conquering England and marked the rise of English naval power.
A small Moroccan force armed with firearms crossed the Sahara and destroyed the Songhai Empire at the Battle of Tondibi, ending West Africa's greatest imperial tradition.
A small Moroccan force with firearms crossed the Sahara and destroyed the Songhai Empire at Tondibi. Ended the last great West African imperial tradition.
Hideyoshi invaded Korea as a stepping stone to China. Korean admiral Yi Sun-sin's naval victories and Ming Chinese intervention repelled the invasion.
Succession crisis after the Rurik dynasty ended, with famine, civil war, and Polish-Swedish intervention. Ended with the founding of the Romanov dynasty in 1613.
The decisive battle that ended Japan's Sengoku period of civil war. Tokugawa Ieyasu's victory led to the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate and 250 years of peace.
Mughal persecution of Sikhs from the execution of Guru Arjan through the martyrdom of Guru Tegh Bahadur to the Khalsa uprising. Forged Sikh martial identity.
Poland invaded Russia during the Time of Troubles, occupying Moscow from 1610-1612. The Russian liberation of Moscow became a foundational national myth.
The Tokugawa Shogunate destroyed the last Toyotomi resistance at Osaka Castle, completing the unification of Japan and ushering in the Edo period of peace.
Devastating religious and political war across Central Europe. Killed roughly a third of the German population. Ended with the Peace of Westphalia, founding the modern state system.
The Manchu Qing dynasty conquered Ming China in one of history's deadliest conquests. Wars, massacres, and famine killed tens of millions.
The Dutch occupied northeastern Brazil for 24 years, seeking to control the sugar trade. Portuguese-Brazilian forces eventually expelled them in a prolonged guerrilla campaign.

canonical_name: War of the Three Kingdoms (Britain & Ireland)
summary: Interconnected civil wars across England, Scotland, and Ireland. Cromwell's conquest of Ireland was particularly devastating, with massacres at Drogheda and Wexford.
War between Parliament and King Charles I over governance and religion. Led to the king's execution and Cromwell's Protectorate.
Bohdan Khmelnytsky led a Cossack revolt against Polish rule in Ukraine, massacring tens of thousands of Jews and Poles. Led Ukraine into Russian orbit.
The Mughals and Safavids fought over Kandahar, a strategically vital city between their empires. The Safavids ultimately retained it despite repeated Mughal sieges.
Ottoman invasion of Poland and the heroic defense under Jan Sobieski. Polish victories preserved the Commonwealth's Ukrainian territories and made Sobieski king.

Metacom (King Philip) led a coalition of Native American tribes against New England colonists in one of the deadliest wars in American history per capita. Half the towns in New England were attacked, and the Native population was devastated.
The Marathas waged guerrilla warfare against Mughal emperor Aurangzeb for 27 years. The protracted war exhausted the Mughal Empire and hastened its decline.
A combined Habsburg-Polish relief force under Jan Sobieski routed the Ottoman besiegers in the largest cavalry charge in history. Began the permanent Ottoman retreat from Europe.
Major European coalition war that pushed the Ottomans out of Hungary and much of the Balkans. The Treaty of Karlowitz marked the beginning of Ottoman territorial decline.
European coalition war against Louis XIV's French expansion. Fought across Europe, Ireland, and the colonies. Ended with the Treaty of Ryswick and no clear winner.
Coalition war led by Russia against Sweden. Ended Swedish dominance of the Baltic and established Russia as a major European power under Peter the Great.
European war over who would succeed to the Spanish throne. Resulted in a balance of power, British rise, and the Treaty of Utrecht.
North American theatre of the War of the Spanish Succession. Featured frontier raids, the Deerfield massacre, and the British capture of Acadia (Nova Scotia).
Communities of escaped slaves in Jamaica's mountains fought the British for freedom. The Maroons won a peace treaty granting autonomy — rare in the history of slavery.
European powers fought over Maria Theresa's right to the Habsburg throne. Prussia seized Silesia, setting the stage for the Seven Years War.
The last Stuart attempt to reclaim the British throne. Bonnie Prince Charlie reached Derby before retreating. The Jacobites were destroyed at Culloden, ending Highland clan culture.
North American theatre of the Seven Years War. British victory at Quebec ended French rule in Canada and set the stage for the American Revolution.
Global conflict fought across Europe, the Americas, Africa, and Asia. Often called the first true world war. Britain emerged as the dominant colonial power.
Robert Clive's victory over the Nawab of Bengal established British control over the richest province in India. The beginning of the British Raj.
One of the largest and bloodiest 18th-century battles. The Afghan victory broke Maratha power in northern India, creating a power vacuum the British would fill.
Russia's century-long conquest and ethnic cleansing of the Circassian people from the Northwest Caucasus. Up to 1.5 million Circassians were killed or expelled to the Ottoman Empire.

parties.name: Kingdom of Mysore (Hyder Ali / Tipu Sultan)

summary: Four wars between Britain and the Kingdom of Mysore ended with Tipu Sultan's death at the siege of Seringapatam in 1799, removing Britain's most formidable Indian rival.

Yemelyan Pugachev, claiming to be Tsar Peter III, led the largest peasant revolt in Russian history across the Ural region. Cossacks, serfs, and Bashkirs joined the uprising before it was brutally suppressed. Pugachev was executed in Moscow.
American colonies revolted against British rule, establishing the United States. French intervention proved decisive in the British defeat at Yorktown.
Three wars between Britain and the Maratha Confederacy. British victory in 1818 ended the most powerful indigenous Indian state and established British paramountcy.

Over 150 years of colonial violence against Aboriginal Australians as British settlement expanded across the continent. Hundreds of massacres, poisonings, and punitive expeditions decimated Indigenous populations. The true death toll remains contested and under-studied.
Enslaved people in the French colony of Saint-Domingue overthrew their enslavers, defeated Napoleon's army, and founded the first Black republic. The only successful slave revolution in history.
Revolutionary France fought successive coalitions of European monarchies. The wars spread revolutionary ideals across Europe and set the stage for Napoleon.
The final partition erased Poland from the map of Europe for 123 years. The Kosciuszko Uprising of 1794 failed to prevent Poland's dismemberment by its three neighbours.
Napoleon invaded Egypt to threaten British India. The campaign failed militarily but launched Egyptology (Rosetta Stone) and began the modernisation of the Middle East.
The new United States fought North African pirate states to end tribute payments and protect American shipping. Established the US Navy's reputation and the Marine Corps anthem.
Napoleon's campaigns reshaped Europe's borders and spread revolutionary principles. Ended with his defeat at Waterloo and the Congress of Vienna.
Russia conquered the Caucasus from Persia, taking modern Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Dagestan. The Treaty of Gulistan was a humiliating loss for Persia.
Napoleon's masterpiece. Defeated the combined Austro-Russian army by feigning weakness on his right, then counterattacking through the centre. Ended the Third Coalition.
Nelson's decisive naval victory off the Spanish coast destroyed the Franco-Spanish fleet and secured British naval supremacy for a century. Nelson was killed in the battle.
Napoleon destroyed the Prussian army in a single day in twin battles, leading to the occupation of Berlin. Humiliated Prussia into profound military and social reforms.
Napoleon's occupation of Spain triggered a brutal guerrilla war that tied down hundreds of thousands of French troops. Called the 'Spanish Ulcer' by Napoleon.
Introduction of European muskets to New Zealand triggered devastating intertribal warfare. Tribes with guns conquered those without, killing up to 40,000 and reshaping Maori society.
Wave of revolutions across Spanish America led by Simón Bolívar and José de San Martín, creating independent nations from Mexico to Argentina.
Ottoman-Egyptian forces destroyed the First Saudi State and the Wahhabi movement's capital at Diriyah. The Wahhabis would later revive under Ibn Saud in the 20th century.

Shawnee leader Tecumseh built a pan-Indian confederacy to resist American westward expansion. The movement was dealt a blow at Tippecanoe in 1811, and Tecumseh was killed fighting alongside the British at the Battle of the Thames in 1813.
Bloodiest single day of the Napoleonic Wars, fought before Moscow. A pyrrhic French victory that failed to destroy the Russian army, dooming the invasion.

parties.name: British Empire / Canadian militia / Indigenous allies

summary: American forces invaded British North America in multiple campaigns, including battles at Queenston Heights and the burning of York (Toronto). British-Canadian-Indigenous defenders repelled the invasions, shaping a distinct Canadian national identity.
The 'Battle of the Nations' was the largest battle before WWI. Napoleon's decisive defeat by the allied coalition forced his retreat from Germany and led to his first abdication.

A civil war within the Creek Nation that drew in the United States under Andrew Jackson. The Red Stick faction's defeat at Horseshoe Bend resulted in the Treaty of Fort Jackson, ceding 23 million acres of Creek land.
Napoleon's final defeat by allied forces near Brussels. Ended 23 years of almost continuous warfare in Europe.
Shaka Zulu's military innovations caused the Mfecane — a massive wave of displacement and warfare across southern and eastern Africa that reorganised entire populations.
Three wars to remove the Seminole people from Florida. The costliest Indian war in US history, the Seminoles were never fully conquered and some remain in the Everglades.
Russia's 47-year conquest of the Caucasus, including Imam Shamil's legendary resistance. Ended with the mass expulsion of Circassians — one of the 19th century's largest ethnic cleansings.
Greek revolt against Ottoman rule, supported by Britain, France, and Russia. Established the modern Greek state and inspired nationalist movements across Europe.
Four wars between Britain and the powerful Asante kingdom in West Africa. The Asante resisted tenaciously but were finally conquered in 1900, ending a three-century empire.

parties.name: United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata

summary: Argentina and Brazil fought over the Banda Oriental. British mediation led to the creation of Uruguay as an independent buffer state between the two powers.
Russian victory in the Balkans and Caucasus led to Greek independence and Russian gains in the Caucasus. Further weakened Ottoman control over its European territories.

Sauk leader Black Hawk led a band of warriors and families back across the Mississippi to reclaim their homeland in Illinois. The US Army and militia pursued and massacred them at Bad Axe. Abraham Lincoln briefly served as a militia captain.
Three civil wars in Spain between liberal constitutionalists and Catholic traditionalist Carlists over succession and ideology. Foreshadowed the Spanish Civil War.

Governor Stirling led a punitive expedition against the Binjareb Noongar people at the Murray River in Western Australia. Official reports claimed 15 dead, but Aboriginal accounts suggest up to 80 killed including women and children.

Stockmen murdered at least 28 unarmed Wirrayaraay Aboriginal people at Myall Creek station in New South Wales. Unusually, seven perpetrators were tried and hanged, one of the rare cases of colonial justice for frontier violence.
Britain waged war to force China to accept opium imports. China's defeat led to the cession of Hong Kong and the Century of Humiliation.
Two wars between Britain and the powerful Sikh Empire of the Punjab. British victory annexed the Punjab and the Koh-i-Noor diamond, completing British dominance of India.
Series of wars between British settlers and Maori over land and sovereignty in New Zealand. Maori military engineering impressed the British, but settler numbers prevailed.
The US invaded Mexico and seized half its territory including California, Texas, and the Southwest. A pivotal expansion of US continental power.

parties.name: Austrian Empire / Papal States / Kingdom of Two Sicilies

summary: Garibaldi, Cavour, and Victor Emmanuel unified the Italian peninsula through diplomacy and war. Created the modern Italian nation-state from a patchwork of kingdoms.
Decades of guerrilla warfare by Apache bands against American and Mexican forces in the Southwest. Geronimo's surrender in 1886 marked the end of the major Indian Wars.
Massive civil war in China led by a Christian mystic. The deadliest civil war in history, killing more people than World War I. Devastated southern China.
War fought primarily in Crimea between Russia and an alliance of Ottoman, British, French, and Sardinian forces. Notable for terrible logistics and Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms.
Mounted rebel movement in northern China fighting simultaneously with the Taiping Rebellion. The combined rebellions nearly toppled the Qing dynasty.
Year-long siege of the Russian naval base at Sevastopol. Featured the Charge of the Light Brigade and Florence Nightingale's nursing innovations. Half the casualties were from disease.
Miao ethnic uprisings in southwestern China during the Taiping era. The Qing suppression was devastating, killing a large portion of Guizhou's population.
Britain and France defeated China again, burning the Summer Palace in Beijing. Forced further 'unequal treaties' that opened more of China to foreign exploitation.
Muslim uprising in Yunnan province that established an independent sultanate. The Qing reconquest was brutal, killing much of the province's Muslim population.
Widespread uprising against British rule in India. Though suppressed, it ended Company rule and established direct British Crown control over India.
The British Residency at Lucknow was besieged for 87 days during the Indian Rebellion. Its relief became an iconic episode of British imperial mythology.
War between the Union and seceding Southern states over slavery and states' rights. The deadliest conflict in American history, ending slavery in the United States.
Decades of warfare between the US and Native American nations of the Great Plains, from the Dakota War through Little Bighorn to the Wounded Knee massacre in 1890.
Massive Muslim rebellion in northwestern China, one of the deadliest conflicts in history by death toll. Qing reconquest devastated the region's population.
Turning point of the American Civil War. Three-day battle in Pennsylvania ended Lee's invasion of the North and marked the beginning of the Confederacy's decline.

canonical_name: Paraguayan War (War of the Triple Alliance)
summary: Paraguay fought Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay in South America's deadliest war. Paraguay lost 60-90% of its male population in the catastrophic defeat.
Bismarck engineered three wars — against Denmark, Austria, and France — to unify the German states under Prussian leadership, creating the German Empire in 1871.

Colorado militia under Colonel Chivington attacked a peaceful Cheyenne and Arapaho camp at Sand Creek, killing and mutilating around 150 people, mostly women and children. The massacre provoked outrage even at the time and triggered wider Plains warfare.
Prussia and Austria defeated Denmark over the duchies of Schleswig and Holstein. Denmark lost a third of its territory and population. The war was Bismarck's first step toward German unification.

canonical_name: Conquest of the Khanate of Kokand
summary: Russia conquered Central Asia's Kokand Khanate, part of the 'Great Game' rivalry with Britain. Established Russian control over modern Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan.

Oglala Lakota chief Red Cloud led a successful campaign against the US Army along the Bozeman Trail in Wyoming and Montana. The Fetterman Fight saw 81 soldiers killed. The war ended with the Fort Laramie Treaty, one of the few Native American military victories.
The brief civil war that ended the Tokugawa Shogunate and restored imperial rule under Emperor Meiji. Launched Japan's rapid modernization from feudalism to industrial power.

Louis Riel led the Metis people of the Red River Settlement in resisting Canadian annexation of Rupert's Land. The rebellion resulted in the creation of Manitoba as a province, though Riel was forced into exile.
Prussian victory that unified Germany, ended the French Second Empire, and seeded Franco-German enmity that would drive both World Wars.
War to force Sioux onto reservations following the Black Hills gold rush. Included Custer's Last Stand at Little Bighorn, where Sitting Bull's warriors wiped out the 7th Cavalry.
Russia intervened to support Balkan Christians against Ottoman rule. Victory led to Bulgarian independence and the Congress of Berlin, reshaping the Balkans.
Last stand of the samurai class against Meiji modernization. Saigo Takamori's samurai were defeated by conscript peasant soldiers, ending the feudal warrior tradition in Japan.

Chief Joseph led the Nez Perce on a 1,170-mile fighting retreat toward Canada to escape forced relocation. They fought and won several engagements before being surrounded just 40 miles from the border. Joseph's surrender speech became iconic.
British invasion of the Zulu Kingdom in southern Africa. Despite a shocking British defeat at Isandlwana, the Zulu state was destroyed within months.
Chile defeated Bolivia and Peru to seize the nitrate-rich Atacama Desert. Bolivia lost its entire Pacific coastline, a grievance still unresolved today.
Islamist revolt in Sudan overthrew Egyptian rule and killed General Gordon at Khartoum. Ended with Kitchener's reconquest at Omdurman.

parties.name: Nguyen dynasty / Black Flags / Qing China

summary: France conquered northern Vietnam (Tonkin) despite resistance from Vietnamese, Chinese, and Black Flag irregulars. Established French Indochina, sowing seeds for later conflicts.
France fought China over control of Vietnam. Despite Chinese victories on land, French naval superiority prevailed, establishing French control over Indochina.

parties.name: Metis / Cree / Assiniboine (Louis Riel / Big Bear / Poundmaker)

summary: Louis Riel returned from exile to lead a second Metis uprising in Saskatchewan, joined by Cree and Assiniboine allies. The rebellion was crushed by Canadian troops transported on the new transcontinental railway. Riel was hanged for treason.

US soldiers massacred around 300 Lakota men, women, and children at Wounded Knee Creek in South Dakota while attempting to disarm them. Considered the last major event of the American Indian Wars and a symbol of the destruction of Native American life.
Japan's decisive victory over China revealed the Qing dynasty's weakness and established Japan as East Asia's dominant military power.
Korean peasant uprising against corrupt officials and foreign influence. Its suppression by Japan and China triggered the First Sino-Japanese War.
Ethiopia defeated an Italian invasion at the Battle of Adwa, the most significant African victory over European colonialism. Ethiopia remained independent during the Scramble for Africa.
French and British forces confronted each other at Fashoda in Sudan during the Scramble for Africa. France backed down, averting war but cementing British control of the Nile.
British forces armed with machine guns and artillery destroyed the Mahdist army in Sudan. Demonstrated the devastating power gap between European and non-European armies.
British war against the Boer republics of southern Africa. Notorious for Britain's use of concentration camps, which killed tens of thousands of Boer civilians.
Anti-foreign Chinese militia besieged foreign legations in Beijing. An international force of eight nations intervened, further humiliating the Qing dynasty.

After Spain ceded the Philippines, Filipinos fought for independence from their new American colonizers. Brutal counterinsurgency tactics and civilian deaths marked America's first overseas colonial war.
Ibn Saud conquered the Arabian Peninsula using Wahhabist Ikhwan warriors, unifying it as Saudi Arabia by 1932. He then crushed the Ikhwan when they resisted modernisation.
Japan's victory over Russia shocked the world and ended Russian expansion in East Asia. The first modern war won by an Asian power against a European one.
German colonial forces systematically exterminated the Herero and Nama peoples of South-West Africa. Considered the first genocide of the 20th century.
Major indigenous uprising against German colonial forced-labour cotton farming in Tanganyika. Crushed with scorched-earth tactics that killed hundreds of thousands through famine.
Iran's first attempt at democratic government, establishing a parliament. Russian intervention helped the Shah crush the movement, but it shaped Iran's political consciousness.
A decade of revolution and civil war in Mexico involving multiple factions. Pancho Villa and Emiliano Zapata became folk heroes. Resulted in the 1917 constitution.
Italy seized Libya and the Dodecanese islands from the Ottoman Empire. Featured the first-ever aerial bombing in warfare and encouraged the Balkan Wars.
Balkan states expelled the Ottoman Empire from almost all of its remaining European territory. Redrew the map of southeastern Europe.
Bulgaria attacked its former allies over the division of Macedonia but was defeated on all fronts. Created lasting Balkan resentments that fed into WWI.
Four years of trench warfare across northern France and Belgium. The Somme, Verdun, Passchendaele — a generation destroyed in a strip of mud.
Mobile warfare across Poland, the Baltics, and Ukraine. Russian defeats fuelled the revolution that ended the Tsarist empire.
French and British forces halted the German advance toward Paris, ending the war of movement and beginning four years of trench warfare.
Germany's decisive encirclement and destruction of a Russian army in East Prussia. The disaster ended Russia's invasion of Germany and made Hindenburg a national hero.

canonical_name: World War I – Middle Eastern Theatre
summary: British and Arab forces fought the Ottoman Empire across Mesopotamia, Palestine, and Arabia. T.E. Lawrence's Arab Revolt and the fall of Jerusalem reshaped the modern Middle East.
British-Indian forces fought up the Tigris and Euphrates, suffering a humiliating surrender at Kut in 1916 before capturing Baghdad in 1917.
German commander Lettow-Vorbeck led a brilliant guerrilla campaign across East Africa with a small force, never surrendering. Most casualties were African porters and civilians.

German forces committed widespread atrocities against Belgian civilians during their invasion, killing over 5,000 and destroying the university library of Leuven. Allied propaganda amplified but did not invent the events.
Failed Allied attempt to knock the Ottoman Empire out of WWI by seizing the Dardanelles. A defining national moment for Australia, New Zealand, and Turkey.
Systematic extermination of Armenians by the Ottoman government through massacres, death marches, and forced starvation. The first genocide of the 20th century.
One of the bloodiest battles in history. Britain suffered 57,000 casualties on the first day alone. Epitomised the futility of WWI trench warfare.
Germany's attrition offensive against the French fortress city of Verdun. Ten months of fighting produced nearly a million casualties with minimal territorial change.
The only major fleet engagement of WWI. Britain suffered heavier losses but maintained its naval blockade of Germany. Germany never again challenged the Grand Fleet.
The most successful Allied offensive of WWI on any front. Russia shattered the Austro-Hungarian army, but the staggering casualties hastened the Russian Revolution.
After Yuan Shikai's death, China fragmented into warlord-controlled regions fighting constantly for territory. Chiang Kai-shek's Northern Expedition nominally reunified the country.

Central Asian Muslim populations resisted first Tsarist and then Soviet rule in a protracted guerrilla war across modern Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan. Soviet collectivisation and anti-religious policies fuelled the resistance.
British offensive in Flanders, fought in apocalyptic mud. Gained five miles at the cost of half a million casualties. Synonymous with the horror of WWI.
Multi-sided civil war following the Bolshevik Revolution. Combat, famine, and disease killed millions and established the Soviet Union.
German-Austrian forces smashed the Italian front in a devastating breakthrough. Italy lost 300,000 prisoners but rallied to hold the Piave line. Hemingway served here.
Germany's last gamble to win WWI before American troops arrived in force. Initial breakthroughs failed to achieve decisive results, exhausting Germany's reserves.
The final Allied offensive that broke the Hindenburg Line and ended WWI. Combined arms tactics with tanks, aircraft, and infantry proved decisive.

Following Finnish independence from Russia, a brief but brutal civil war erupted between the conservative White Guard and socialist Red Guard. The Whites won with German support. Thousands of Reds died in prison camps from starvation and disease.
Greece invaded Anatolia to claim territory promised by the Allies. Turkish forces under Atatürk expelled the Greeks, leading to massive population exchanges.
Poland defeated a Soviet invasion at the 'Miracle on the Vistula' near Warsaw in 1920, halting the spread of Bolshevism into western Europe.
Irish guerrilla campaign against British rule led by Michael Collins. Resulted in the Anglo-Irish Treaty and the creation of the Irish Free State.
Berber resistance to Spanish colonialism in Morocco's Rif mountains. Abd el-Krim inflicted the devastating defeat of Annual on Spain before French intervention crushed the republic.
The compulsory population exchange between Greece and Turkey uprooted 1.5 million Greeks and 500,000 Turks. Accompanied by violence, it was a landmark in ethnic cleansing.
Catholic uprising against Mexico's anticlerical laws. A bloody religious war in rural Mexico that killed tens of thousands and was resolved through church-state negotiations.

The last officially acknowledged frontier massacre in Australia. Police and settlers killed dozens of Aboriginal people in central Australia in retaliation for the death of a white dingo trapper. A subsequent inquiry whitewashed the killings.
Bolivia and Paraguay fought over the Gran Chaco region, believed to hold oil. Paraguay won despite being smaller and poorer. The deadliest South American war of the 20th century.
Stalin's forced collectivisation caused a devastating famine in Ukraine, killing millions. Many nations now recognise it as genocide, a deliberate attempt to crush Ukrainian identity.
Fascist Italy invaded Ethiopia using poison gas and aerial bombing against a poorly-equipped army. The League of Nations' failure to act fatally undermined collective security.
Fascist-backed Nationalists overthrew the Spanish Republic with German and Italian support. A rehearsal for WWII, including the bombing of Guernica.
Palestinian uprising against British rule and Jewish immigration. Britain crushed the revolt with collective punishment, demolishing homes and detaining thousands.
Japan's brutal invasion of China, including the Nanjing Massacre. Merged into WWII and resulted in massive Chinese military and civilian casualties.
Japanese troops massacred civilians and prisoners of war after capturing the Chinese capital of Nanjing. One of the worst atrocities of the 20th century.
History's deadliest conflict. Nazi Germany's conquest of Europe and the Allied response, from Blitzkrieg through D-Day to the fall of Berlin.
Soviet invasion of Finland met fierce resistance. Finnish ski troops inflicted massive casualties on the Red Army, exposing Soviet military weakness that encouraged Hitler.
Soviet forces under Zhukov decisively defeated Japan on the Mongolian border. Japan's defeat convinced it to expand south (Pearl Harbor) rather than north against the USSR.
Germany and the Soviet Union invaded Poland from west and east, triggering World War II. Poland was conquered in five weeks despite fierce resistance.
Germany's air campaign to achieve air superiority over Britain failed. The RAF's defence prevented a German invasion and was the first major Axis defeat.
German blitzkrieg through the Ardennes bypassed the Maginot Line and conquered France in six weeks. The Dunkirk evacuation saved the British Expeditionary Force.
The most bombed place in WWII. Axis forces attempted to neutralise the strategically vital island with over 3,000 bombing raids. Malta held, earning the George Cross.
Soviet secret police murdered 22,000 Polish officers, police, and intellectuals in the Katyn Forest. The USSR denied responsibility until 1990. A foundational Polish-Russian grievance.

Germany invaded Norway to secure iron ore supply routes and outflank Britain. Despite fierce Norwegian resistance and an Allied counter-landing at Narvik, Norway fell in two months. The government fled to London and maintained a government-in-exile.
872-day German siege of Leningrad. Over a million civilians died, mostly from starvation. One of history's most destructive sieges and a symbol of Soviet resilience.
Systematic, state-sponsored genocide of six million Jews and millions of others by Nazi Germany. Auschwitz, Treblinka, and other camps industrialised mass murder.
War across the Pacific from Pearl Harbor to Hiroshima. Island-hopping campaigns, firebombing of Japanese cities, and the first use of nuclear weapons.
Australian troops held Tobruk against Rommel's siege for 241 days, the first time Axis forces were stopped in the North African campaign. Known as the 'Rats of Tobruk.'
The largest military operation in history — 3.8 million Axis troops invaded the Soviet Union on a 1,800-mile front. Initial success was halted at the gates of Moscow.
Finland fought alongside Germany to reclaim territory lost in the Winter War. Finland eventually switched sides and expelled German forces from Lapland.
The turning point of WWII in Europe. The Soviet encirclement and destruction of the German 6th Army was the deadliest battle in human history.
British victory in North Africa that ended the Axis threat to Egypt and the Suez Canal. Churchill said: 'Before Alamein we never had a victory. After Alamein we never had a defeat.'
US Navy sank four Japanese aircraft carriers in the decisive battle of the Pacific War. Shifted the strategic initiative permanently to the Allies.
First major Allied offensive in the Pacific. Six months of brutal fighting on land, sea, and air ended Japanese expansion and began the Allied island-hopping campaign.
The 'Forgotten War' in the jungles of Burma. Japanese forces were repelled at Imphal and Kohima in 1944, followed by a massive British-Indian reconquest.
Round-the-clock Allied bombing devastated German cities and industry. The firebombing of Dresden and Hamburg caused massive civilian casualties, debated to this day.
Japanese forces marched 75,000 American and Filipino POWs 65 miles in scorching heat without food or water. Thousands died of exhaustion, disease, and brutality.

Japanese carrier aircraft launched two massive air raids on Darwin, the largest foreign attack on Australian soil. The raids sank eight ships and destroyed most of the town's infrastructure. The Australian government initially censored news of the attack's severity.

Australian forces fought a gruelling campaign along the Kokoda Track in Papua New Guinea to prevent Japanese capture of Port Moresby. The jungle fighting in extreme conditions became a defining moment in Australian military history.

A predominantly Canadian amphibious assault on the German-held port of Dieppe in France. The raid was a catastrophic failure, with nearly 60% of Canadian troops killed, wounded, or captured. Lessons learned shaped planning for D-Day.

Japan captured the supposedly impregnable British fortress of Singapore in a stunning 10-day campaign, taking 80,000 prisoners. Churchill called it the worst disaster in British military history. It shattered the myth of European invincibility in Asia.
Largest tank battle in history. Soviet defensive victory that permanently seized the strategic initiative on the Eastern Front.
Man-made famine in British Bengal caused by wartime policies and deliberate diversion of food. Churchill's refusal to send relief contributed to 2-3 million deaths.
The largest amphibious invasion in history. Allied forces landed on five beaches in Normandy, opening the Western Front and beginning the liberation of France.
Germany's last major offensive in the west, through the Ardennes forest. Initial surprise gains were reversed as Allied forces held and counterattacked.
Japan's attempted invasion of India was defeated in northeastern India. Called 'Britain's greatest battle' — the turning point of the war in Southeast Asia.
The largest naval battle in history by number of ships. Destroyed the Japanese navy's ability to fight and introduced kamikaze attacks as Japan grew desperate.
Four bloody battles to break through the German Gustav Line in Italy. The ancient monastery was controversially bombed. Polish troops finally captured the summit.
Allied amphibious landing behind German lines in Italy that failed to achieve a breakout for four months. Intended to bypass the Gustav Line, it became a costly siege.
American B-29s firebombed Japanese cities, destroying 67 cities. The Tokyo firebombing of March 1945 killed more people than either atomic bomb.
Stalin deported the entire Chechen and Ingush populations to Central Asia, accusing them of collaboration with the Nazis. A quarter to a third died. They returned only in 1957.
Soviet forces stormed Berlin in the final battle of the European war. Hitler committed suicide in his bunker as Soviet flags were raised over the Reichstag.
Brutal 36-day battle for a tiny volcanic island. Of 21,000 Japanese defenders, only 216 survived. The iconic flag-raising became a symbol of American resolve.
Largest amphibious battle of the Pacific. Massive civilian casualties and fierce Japanese resistance influenced the decision to use atomic bombs.
The first nuclear weapon used in warfare destroyed Hiroshima on August 6, 1945. Combined with Nagasaki three days later, it forced Japan's surrender.
Second and last nuclear weapon used in warfare destroyed Nagasaki on August 9, 1945, three days after Hiroshima.
Communist forces under Mao Zedong defeated the Nationalists, establishing the People's Republic of China. The KMT retreated to Taiwan.
Soviet forces destroyed the Japanese Kwantung Army in Manchuria in just two weeks. Contributed to Japan's decision to surrender alongside the atomic bombs.
Vietnamese independence war against French colonial rule. France's defeat at Dien Bien Phu ended European colonialism in Indochina.
First proxy war of the Cold War. Communist guerrillas fought the Western-backed government. The Truman Doctrine was created to support Greece, setting Cold War precedent.
The partition of British India into India and Pakistan triggered one of history's largest mass migrations and horrific sectarian violence on both sides.
Arab states invaded the newly declared State of Israel. Israel survived and expanded its territory, while 700,000 Palestinians became refugees.
Communist guerrilla war in British Malaya. One of the few successful counterinsurgencies, using hearts-and-minds tactics alongside resettlement programmes.
Communist insurgency in British Malaya, one of the few successful counterinsurgencies. British 'hearts and minds' strategy combined with resettlement defeated the guerrillas.
North Korean invasion of the South drew in US/UN and Chinese forces. Ended in stalemate near the original border, leaving Korea divided to this day.
China sent 300,000 troops across the Yalu River, driving UN forces back from the Chinese border to south of Seoul. The Chosin Reservoir battle became a legendary Marine Corps retreat.
Kikuyu-led uprising against British colonial rule in Kenya. Brutal British suppression included concentration camps, but accelerated Kenyan independence.
Brutal guerrilla war for Algerian independence from France. French use of torture and mass displacement marked one of decolonisation's bloodiest conflicts.
Viet Minh forces besieged and overran the French garrison in a remote valley. France's most humiliating colonial defeat ended 90 years of French rule in Indochina.
Cold War proxy conflict in which the US intervened to prevent communist unification of Vietnam. American withdrawal in 1973 led to North Vietnamese victory in 1975.
Britain, France, and Israel invaded Egypt after Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal. US and Soviet pressure forced withdrawal, ending British and French influence in the Middle East.
Soviet tanks crushed the Hungarian Revolution that sought to leave the Warsaw Pact. Over 200,000 Hungarians fled the country. Showed the limits of de-Stalinisation.
Hungarians revolted against Soviet-imposed rule, briefly establishing an independent government. Soviet tanks crushed the revolution. 200,000 Hungarians fled the country.
Tibetan uprising against Chinese rule was crushed by the PLA. The Dalai Lama fled to India. Marked the definitive end of Tibetan autonomy.
The 'Secret War' in Laos saw massive US bombing — more bombs than all of WWII combined — while communist Pathet Lao forces gradually took over the country.
Chaotic civil war following Congo's independence from Belgium. Lumumba was assassinated, Katanga seceded, and Mobutu eventually seized power.
Central America's longest civil war featured genocide against Mayan communities by the military. A UN truth commission found the state responsible for 93% of atrocities.
Thirty-year Eritrean war of independence from Ethiopia, one of Africa's longest conflicts. Resulted in Eritrean independence in 1993.
Thirty-year guerrilla war for Eritrean independence from Ethiopia. One of Africa's longest conflicts, ending with Eritrean independence in 1993.
Portugal fought to retain its African colonies for 13 years. The unsustainable wars led to the Carnation Revolution in 1974, ending both the wars and Portugal's dictatorship.
China launched a surprise invasion across the disputed Himalayan border, defeating Indian forces. China withdrew after achieving its territorial objectives, humiliating India.
Communist insurgency in southern Oman supported by South Yemen. Sultan Qaboos overthrew his father and, with British and Iranian help, defeated the rebels in a successful counterinsurgency.
Nomadic Tuareg people fought for independence or autonomy in the Sahara across multiple rebellions. The 2012 rebellion, exploiting Libya's collapse, briefly created an independent Azawad.
Indigenous Papuans have fought for independence since Indonesia took control from the Netherlands. Alleged genocide, resource extraction, and transmigration policies fuel the conflict.
Black nationalist guerrillas fought the white-minority Rhodesian government. Led to the Lancaster House Agreement and the creation of Zimbabwe.
Half-century Marxist insurgency in Colombia involving guerrillas, drug cartels, and right-wing paramilitaries. A 2016 peace deal ended most fighting, earning a Nobel Peace Prize.
African majority overthrew the Arab-dominated Sultan of Zanzibar. Up to 20,000 Arabs were killed and thousands more expelled. Zanzibar merged with Tanganyika to form Tanzania.
Second war between India and Pakistan over Kashmir. Largest tank battles since WWII, ending in UN-brokered ceasefire with no territorial change.
Following an alleged communist coup, the Indonesian army and civilian militias massacred suspected communists and ethnic Chinese. One of the 20th century's worst mass killings, long hidden from view.
Decades of civil war in Chad involving northern Muslim and southern Christian factions, with Libyan intervention. The Toyota War of 1987 saw Chad defeat Libya's modern army with pickup trucks.
South Africa fought to maintain control over Namibia while intervening in the Angolan Civil War. The Battle of Cuito Cuanavale in 1988 was a turning point.
Israel launched pre-emptive strikes against Arab neighbours, capturing the Sinai, West Bank, Gaza, and Golan Heights in six days. Reshaped the Middle East permanently.
Biafra's secession from Nigeria led to civil war and a devastating blockade. Mass starvation killed more people than combat, particularly children.
The Khmer Rouge gradually conquered Cambodia with North Vietnamese support, defeating the US-backed government. Their victory led directly to the Cambodian genocide.
India's longest-running internal conflict. Maoist guerrillas control parts of central-eastern India's 'Red Corridor,' fighting for tribal rights and against economic inequality.

canonical_name: Prague Spring — Soviet invasion of Czechoslovakia
summary: Warsaw Pact forces invaded Czechoslovakia to crush Alexander Dubcek's liberalisation programme ('socialism with a human face'). Ended hopes of reforming communism from within.
Massive surprise North Vietnamese offensive during the Tet holiday. A military failure but a political turning point — American public opinion turned against the war.
Three decades of sectarian violence in Northern Ireland between Catholic nationalists and Protestant unionists. The Good Friday Agreement of 1998 largely ended the conflict.
Half-century Muslim insurgency in the southern Philippines seeking autonomy or independence. The 2014 peace deal created the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, though ISIS-linked groups persist.
Jordan expelled the PLO after Palestinian militants hijacked planes and threatened the monarchy. The PLO relocated to Lebanon, sowing the seeds of the Lebanese Civil War.
The final Nigerian offensive captured the Biafran capital and ended the secession. Images of starving Biafran children became iconic symbols of humanitarian crisis.
East Pakistan's independence war against West Pakistan, supported by India. Pakistani army committed widespread atrocities before surrendering in December 1971.
India intervened in the Bangladesh Liberation War, defeating Pakistan in just 13 days — the fastest surrender of such scale since WWII. Created the nation of Bangladesh.
Egypt and Syria launched a surprise attack on Israel during Yom Kippur. Initial Arab successes were reversed, but the war led to the Camp David peace accords.
Bangladesh's founding leader was assassinated in a military coup just four years after independence. Series of coups and counter-coups followed amid devastating famine.
Khmer Rouge regime killed roughly a quarter of Cambodia's population through execution, forced labour, and starvation in pursuit of an agrarian utopia.
Multi-sided civil war in Lebanon involving sectarian militias, Israel, Syria, and the PLO. Destroyed Beirut and left deep sectarian divisions.
Cold War proxy conflict in Angola lasting 27 years. Involved Cuban troops, South African forces, and massive landmine contamination.
Indonesia invaded East Timor after Portuguese withdrawal, killing up to a quarter of the population through violence and famine. Independence was achieved in 1999 after a UN referendum.
Morocco occupied Western Sahara after Spain withdrew, fighting the Polisario Front. A ceasefire has held since 1991, but the territory's status remains unresolved.
Devastating proxy war in Mozambique between the Marxist government and RENAMO rebels backed by Rhodesia and apartheid South Africa.
Somalia invaded Ethiopia's Ogaden region but was repelled by Ethiopian forces with massive Soviet and Cuban military support. A dramatic Cold War reversal of alliances.
Devastating proxy war in Mozambique between the Marxist government and RENAMO rebels backed by Rhodesia and apartheid South Africa. Ended by peace agreement in 1992.
The Sandinista revolution overthrew Somoza, then US-backed Contras waged war against the new government. The Iran-Contra scandal revealed illegal US funding.
Tanzania invaded Uganda after Amin attempted to annex the Kagera region. Tanzanian forces toppled Idi Amin's brutal dictatorship, one of Africa's worst.
Vietnam invaded Cambodia, toppling the genocidal Khmer Rouge regime. A rare case of military intervention ending a genocide, though condemned by the international community.
Soviet invasion to prop up a communist government faced fierce guerrilla resistance backed by the US and Pakistan. The USSR's Vietnam, contributing to its collapse.
China invaded Vietnam to 'teach it a lesson' for toppling the Khmer Rouge. A brief, bloody border war where both sides claimed victory, revealing China's military weaknesses.
Marxist guerrillas fought the US-backed government in a brutal civil war marked by death squads, the El Mozote massacre, and assassination of Archbishop Romero.
Eight-year war of attrition between Iraq and revolutionary Iran. Featured trench warfare, chemical weapons, and child soldiers. Ended in exhausted stalemate.
Maoist insurgency in Peru waged a brutal campaign of terror against peasants and the state. The capture of leader Guzman in 1992 broke the movement.
Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands; Britain dispatched a naval task force and retook them in 74 days. A brief, sharp conventional war in the South Atlantic.
Decades-long civil war between Sinhalese-dominated government and Tamil separatists. Ended with the military defeat of the LTTE and allegations of war crimes.
Africa's longest civil war between Sudan's Arab Muslim north and Christian/animist south. Two million died, mostly from famine, before the peace deal that led to South Sudan's independence.
During the Iran-Iraq War, both sides attacked oil tankers in the Persian Gulf. The US Navy intervened to protect Kuwaiti tankers, leading to clashes with Iran.
Sustained Palestinian uprising against Israeli occupation of the West Bank and Gaza. Characterised by stone-throwing, strikes, and civil disobedience. Led to the Oslo Accords.
Joseph Kony's LRA abducted tens of thousands of children as soldiers and sex slaves across central Africa. One of the most notorious rebel groups in modern history.
Charles Taylor's invasion plunged Liberia into a horrific civil war with child soldiers, cannibalism, and wholesale destruction. Destabilised the entire region.
After the Soviet withdrawal, mujahideen factions fought each other until the Taliban captured Kabul in 1996, imposing extreme Islamic rule until the US invasion of 2001.
Ongoing insurgency in Indian-administered Kashmir seeking independence or merger with Pakistan. Massive Indian military deployment, human rights abuses, and periodic crises.
